statusLine: EU fuel reserves are under strain with 20 of 27 countries holding critical levels in at least one fuel type, while oil prices have surged 20.5% since February's Middle East escalation.
fullAnalysis:
European fuel security remains under pressure as strategic reserve releases following Middle East supply disruptions have left most EU countries with depleted stocks. While EU averages for petrol (96.3 days), diesel (88.8 days), and jet fuel (87.3 days) hover near the 90-day mandatory minimum, the distribution reveals significant vulnerabilities across member states.

Twenty of 27 countries are currently holding critical stock levels in at least one fuel category, with several facing acute shortages across multiple fuels. Luxembourg, Poland, Austria, and Romania show the most concerning positions with critical levels across all three fuel types. Poland's jet fuel reserves at just 22.5 days and Portugal's at 21.2 days represent particularly severe shortfalls. Diesel supplies appear most strained overall, with 18 countries below the 90-day threshold.

The 20.5% surge in Brent crude prices from $72.48 to $87.33 per barrel since late February reflects ongoing market tensions from the Middle East crisis. This price pressure is evident at European fuel pumps, with significant variation across member states—Danish consumers face petrol at €2.392 per liter while Malta enjoys prices at €1.34. The combination of elevated oil prices and depleted strategic reserves creates a challenging environment for EU energy security.
keyPoints: 20 of 27 EU countries hold critical fuel stock levels in at least one category, with diesel most affected (18 countries below 90-day minimum)
Four countries—Luxembourg, Poland, Austria, and Romania—face critical shortages across all three fuel types
Jet fuel supplies show extreme strain in several countries, with Poland at 22.5 days and Portugal at 21.2 days
Oil prices have surged 20.5% since February's Middle East escalation, from $72.48 to $87.33 per barrel
Fuel price disparities across the EU remain significant, ranging from €1.34 per liter in Malta to €2.392 in Denmark for petrol
